payload.text:
🌐 ویب ایپ بنا سکیں
📱 اسی ویب کو Android App میں کنورٹ کر سکیں
🤖 ChatGPT, Google Gemini, Groq, Claude سب ایک ہی ایپ میں
🔁 تمام ماڈلز سوئچ کے ساتھ
📥 فری میں ڈاؤنلوڈ ایبل APK / Web App
🧠 آگے چل کر AI سے دوسری ایپس بھی بنوا سکیں
یہ سب ایک ہی پرامپٹ میں 👇
🔥 ماسٹر پرامپٹ (AI Web + App Builder کے لیے)
اس پورے متن کو ایک ہی بار کسی AI App Builder / Web Builder (جیسے Lovable, Cursor, Replit AI, Bolt, Firebase Studio, یا Custom GPT Builder) میں ڈالیں
🎯 PROJECT TITLE
All-in-One Multi-AI Platform (Web + App)
🧠 PROJECT GOAL
ایک ایسی جدید، محفوظ اور تیز AI Web Application بناؤ جو:
ChatGPT (OpenAI)
Google Gemini (تمام ماڈلز)
Groq AI
Claude (Anthropic)
Future AI APIs
کو ایک ہی انٹرفیس میں شامل کرے
اور اسی ویب ایپ کو Android App میں کنورٹ بھی کیا جا سکے۔
🏗️ TECH STACK (لازمی استعمال کرو)
Frontend:
Next.js / React
Tailwind CSS
Dark + Light Mode
Backend:
Node.js (Express یا API Routes)
Python (Optional AI routing کے لیے)
Database:
Firebase Firestore
Firebase Auth
AI Integration Layer:
Unified AI Router System
Model Switcher Logic
Deployment:
Vercel / Firebase Hosting
🤖 AI SYSTEM REQUIREMENTS
1️⃣ Multi-AI Router
ایسا سسٹم بناؤ جو:
User کے منتخب AI کے مطابق API کال کرے
ایک ہی Prompt کو مختلف AI ماڈلز کو بھیج سکے
Error آئے تو Auto Fallback کرے
Copy code
Js
if (model === "chatgpt") callOpenAI();
else if (model === "gemini") callGemini();
else if (model === "groq") callGroq();
else if (model === "claude") callClaude();
2️⃣ Supported Models (Example)
ChatGPT: gpt-4, gpt-4o, gpt-3.5
Gemini: gemini-pro, gemini-1.5
Groq: llama, mixtral
Claude: claude-3
💬 CHAT FEATURES
Real-time Chat UI
Markdown + Code Highlight
Image Prompt Support
Chat History Save
Copy / Export Chat
🔐 AUTH & SECURITY
Firebase Email/Password Login
Google Sign-In
API Keys محفوظ Backend میں
Rate Limiting
User-based Usage Limit
🧩 APP BUILDER FEATURE (خاص فیچر)
ایپ کے اندر ایک “AI App Builder” سیکشن بناؤ جو:
User سے Prompt لے
AI خود:
Web App Code بنائے
React / Flutter / HTML
Generated App:
Zip میں Export
GitHub Ready
🔁 WEB → APP CONVERSION
Step-by-Step Logic (کوڈ میں شامل کرو):
Web App Responsive ہو
Capacitor / PWA Support
Android APK Build Option
Free Download Button
Copy code
Bash
npm run build
npx cap add android
npx cap open android
📥 FREE DOWNLOAD SYSTEM
Guest Mode (Limited)
Free APK Download
Web App Offline Mode
No Forced Payment
🎨 UI REQUIREMENTS
Modern Glassmorphism UI
Fast Animations
Mobile Friendly
Urdu + English Support
📚 DOCUMENTATION AUTO-GENERATE
ایپ خود:
API Docs
User Guide
Developer Mode
Generate کرے۔
🚀 FINAL OUTPUT
AI یہ سب Generate کرے:
✅ Full Source Code
✅ Step-by-Step Setup Guide
✅ Firebase Config
✅ AI API Integration
✅ Android App Conversion Guide
🪜 اب پورا طریقہ (Step-by-Step ایک ہی جگہ)
1️⃣ اوپر دیا گیا ماسٹر پرامپٹ کسی AI Web/App Builder میں ڈالیں
2️⃣ AI خود مکمل Web App کا Code بنائے گا
3️⃣ Firebase Auth + Database Connect کریں
4️⃣ AI APIs Keys Backend میں لگائیں
5️⃣ Web App Deploy کریں
6️⃣ Capacitor کے ذریعے Android App بنائیں
7️⃣ APK Generate کریں
8️⃣ App کے اندر “AI App Builder” سے نئی ایپس بنائیں
9️⃣ Generated Apps Download / Convert کریں



Upar prompt KO coding ko tabdel Karo 
Agar chat GPT API ya KO e our API ke zarorat ho to bata o

**Defining the Application's Scope**

I'm focusing now on defining the scope of this React web application. The core goal is an "All-in-One Multi-AI Platform", so I'm considering modern features like security and speed. The goal is to design the application so that it meets professional standards.


**Refining the Technical Approach**

I'm now zeroing in on the technical approach, specifically the frontend architecture. Considering the "All-in-One" ambition, I'm focusing on React, Tailwind CSS for styling, and dark/light mode functionality. Although Next.js was mentioned, I will remain consistent with my core instructions of using a vanilla React SPA. Since the goal is a self-contained frontend, I will integrate the Gemini API directly from the client and mock the other APIs.


**Clarifying User Requirements**

I've just finished clarifying the user's requirements for the All-in-One Multi-AI Platform. I've broken down the project scope, which includes the AI models (ChatGPT, Gemini, Groq, Claude), the desired tech stack (React, Tailwind CSS, dark/light mode), and essential chat features like real-time UI, markdown support, image prompts, and chat history. I'll mock the backend APIs since I'm focusing on the React frontend and can't build a full backend as per my role. I'll include UI components for Firebase authentication.


**Defining Core App Features**

I'm now diving deeper into the app's features, translating the user's Urdu and English request. The "All-in-One" ambition requires a multi-AI router for selecting services. I'm focusing on the Gemini integration, along with mock APIs for others. Key chat features like real-time UI, markdown, image prompts, and chat history (simulated locally) are essential, with copy/export buttons. I'll mock Firebase auth UI. I will also incorporate an "AI App Builder" feature. I will focus on making the app PWA-ready for app conversion. I'll plan guest mode, APK download, and offline mode.


**Defining Project Scope**

I'm now formulating a more detailed project definition, translating the mixed Urdu/English user request into actionable steps. The core is the "All-in-One Multi-AI Platform," a modern, secure, and fast React web application. I will focus on Gemini integration, with mock APIs for others. Key features include a multi-AI router, chat features (real-time, markdown, image prompts, local chat history), and Firebase UI, as well as an "AI App Builder." I will also ensure PWA compatibility, including APK download.


**Defining the Technical Architecture**

I'm now establishing the technical architecture for this React application, focusing on a robust and scalable frontend. I will use a standard React project structure. I'm focusing on the main components and state management for the core features like real-time chat, API calls (Gemini integration), the AI App Builder feature and the dark/light mode functionality. I will also incorporate placeholder services for other AI integrations.


**Defining the React Component Architecture**

I'm now determining the React component architecture, focused on the key features. I'm prioritizing the main components for the multi-AI chat interface and the AI App Builder. I am integrating components to manage the Gemini API integration, real-time updates and Markdown support, as well as dark/light mode functionality. I am using a three-column layout to organize the different features of the application.


**Defining the Core Application**

I'm now fully immersed in the project's technical details, structuring the React app, and planning key components and features. I'm focusing on a modern, secure, and fast "All-in-One Multi-AI Platform". I've planned the three-column layout, and I will incorporate a Gemini integration, along with placeholder services for other AI APIs. I'm also planning the dark/light mode toggle.


**Initiating Core React Component Design**

I'm starting the React component design, focusing on the essential features requested. I'm prioritizing the main chat interface with markdown support and Gemini API integration, and the AI App Builder feature. I'm consolidating the file count for simplicity. I will also incorporate dark/light mode functionality. I will structure the UI as requested using a three-column layout. I am following my previous decisions for a vanilla React SPA.


**Refining the Component Structure**

I'm now refining the React component structure to ensure modularity and reusability. I'm focusing on key features, including components for the multi-AI chat interface, the AI App Builder feature and the dark/light mode toggle. I have combined mock services for file count simplicity. I'm prioritizing the main components for the multi-AI chat interface and the AI App Builder.


**Finalizing the Component Structure**

I'm now implementing the final touches on the React application's structure. I'm focusing on consolidating the individual component files into larger screen components, as requested. The integration of Gemini API and mock services is complete. I will focus now on the component for the "AI App Builder" and the real-time chat functionality, now combined into the single "ChatScreen" component. I have created detailed UI plans.


**Initiating React App Build**

I'm now starting the React app build, focusing on a clean and modular structure based on the project definition. I've initiated the file creation sequence, beginning with the `metadata.json` and `index.html`. I'm prioritizing the main `ChatScreen` component and `AppBuilderScreen` components, with the AI integration and mock services planned. I will also make the chat UI responsive to different screen sizes.

entries.description: Defines the application's name and description. This metadata provides essential information about the app's purpose.

The main HTML file for the React application. It includes the Tailwind CSS CDN script, configures dark mode support, sets up a root element for the React app, and applies a base background style for the glassmorphism aesthetic.
The entry point of the React application. It uses React 18's `createRoot` API to render the main `App` component into the DOM. This file initializes the entire application.

The main application component. It sets up the overall layout, theme provider for dark/light mode, and state management for switching between the 'Chat' and 'App Builder' modes. It serves as the root of the UI structure.

Contains all shared TypeScript types and enums for the application. This centralizes type definitions for chat messages, AI providers, models, and application modes, ensuring type safety and consistency across components.
Defines constant values used throughout the application, such as the list of available AI models. This helps in maintaining a single source of truth for configuration data.

A custom React hook and context provider for managing the application's theme (dark/light mode). It handles theme persistence in localStorage and applies the appropriate class to the HTML root element.

This service acts as a unified router for all AI model interactions. It contains the primary logic to call the appropriate AI service (real Gemini API or mock services) based on the user's selection. This file is central to the multi-model functionality of the app.

The main component for the AI Chat interface. It manages the chat history, handles user input (including image uploads), orchestrates API calls through the aiService, and displays the conversation in a real-time, streaming manner.

This component provides the UI and logic for the 'AI App Builder' feature. It allows users to input a prompt for an application they want to build, calls the AI service to generate code, and displays the result with syntax highlighting and a copy-to-clipboard functionality.

diagnostics.message: Object literal may only specify known properties, and 'inlineData' does not exist in type '{ text: string; }'.

payload.text:
**Analyzing the Error**

I'm currently focused on pinpointing the root cause of the TypeScript error in `services/aiService.ts`, specifically the 'inlineData' property issue on line 35. I am investigating the expected structure of the object literal and cross-referencing it with the code's intended behavior. This will lead to a targeted resolution.


**Debugging the Structure**

I am now thoroughly examining the expected object literal structure in relation to line 35 of `services/aiService.ts`. The focus is on the `inlineData` property, which TypeScript flags as unrecognized. I am checking the types defined for the object being used on that line, and cross-referencing this with the `ChatMessage` interface to confirm the expected field names. I believe I am close to determining the discrepancy, and will try a fix soon.


**Resolving the Typo**

I have identified the precise source of the TypeScript error. The `unshift` operation on `userMessageParts` attempts to insert an object containing `inlineData`, which is not compatible with the array's defined type, which expects objects with a `text` property. I am formulating a fix to ensure type compatibility.



**Refining the Approach**

I'm now implementing the fix in `services/aiService.ts`. The solution involves updating the type definition for `userMessageParts` to align with the Gemini API's expected 'parts' structure. I'm choosing to define the type inline, as I can't find an appropriate definition in the `@google/genai` library. This is to ensure that the array can accommodate both text and image parts as per API specifications, resolving the type mismatch error.


**Typing the Array**

I have addressed the type mismatch in `userMessageParts` by explicitly defining it to allow either `text` or `inlineData` properties. The updated declaration ensures that the array can accommodate both text and image parts seamlessly, aligning with the Gemini API's expected 'parts' structure. This change fixes the initial TypeScript error.


**Implementing the Fix**

I am now implementing the fix. The approach entails adjusting the type definition of `userMessageParts` to enable compatibility with both text-based and image-based content elements. This will involve using a union type to accommodate objects with either a 'text' property or an 'inlineData' property, resolving the type mismatch.


**Typing the Prompt**

I am now ready to explicitly type `userMessageParts` to resolve the TypeScript error. After reviewing the Gemini API guidelines and the structure of `Part` objects, I am importing the `Part` type from `@google/genai`. I will use this type declaration to ensure compatibility between text and image parts. I believe this will provide the necessary type safety and resolve the error on line 35.


**Typing the Parameters**

I am now ready to implement the fix. I've considered several approaches, and will proceed with explicitly typing `userMessageParts`. After reviewing the expected data structure and API guidelines, I'm choosing to define the type inline to ensure compatibility for multimodal input, specifically for image-text prompts. This aims to resolve the type mismatch.


**Typing the Parameters**

I'm now ready to explicitly type the `userMessageParts` array in `services/aiService.ts`. After several iterations, I am importing the `Part` type from the `@google/genai` library. I am using this approach to provide strong typing and resolve the initial type mismatch error. This will allow for handling both text and image input to the Gemini API.

I've ensured only user messages with images are correctly processed and included in the API request for conversational context.


**Refining the Solution**

This addresses the initial TypeScript error, which arose from the code trying to `unshift` an object with 'inlineData' into an array that expects objects with a 'text' property.

This addresses the initial TypeScript error, which arose from the code trying to `unshift` an object with 'inlineData' into an array that expects objects with a 'text' property.

I am also fixing a bug where messages from the model with an image would be sent to the api. entries.description: Fixes a TypeScript type error when creating a multimodal message by explicitly typing the 'userMessageParts' array. Also removes an unused 'model' variable and corrects the chat history mapping to properly include images from previous user turns.

payload.text: Create a UI element that allows users to select their preferred AI model from a dropdown list. Implement the logic to dynamically switch the API calls based on the user's selection. Ensure the current selection is maintained and clearly displayed to the user.

Of course! I'd be happy to help you implement a model selector for your AI application. It's a crucial feature for giving users control over their experience.

Based on your request, I've made the following enhancements to your application:

1.  **Reusable `ModelSelector` Component**: I've extracted the dropdown UI into its own reusable component, `components/ModelSelector.tsx`. This promotes code reuse and keeps other components cleaner.
2.  **Model Selection in App Builder**: I've added the new `ModelSelector` to the "AI App Builder" screen. Now you can choose which model you want to use for code generation.
3.  **Dynamic AI Routing**: The `aiService` has been upgraded. The code generation logic now dynamically calls the appropriate service (either the real Google Gemini API or a mock service for other providers) based on your selection in the UI. For non-Gemini models, it will generate a placeholder component to demonstrate the functionality.
4.  **Code Refactoring**: The existing "AI Chat" screen has been updated to use the new, shared `ModelSelector` component, ensuring a consistent look and feel across the application.

These changes bring your application closer to the goal of a truly unified, multi-model AI platform. Here are the necessary code updates:

entries.description: Updates the AI service to handle model selection for code generation. The `generateCode` function now acts as a router, calling the real Gemini API or a new mock code generation function based on the selected model's provider. The `generateGeminiCode` function is updated to accept a dynamic model ID.
Refactors the ChatScreen component by removing the local `ModelSelector` definition and importing the new, reusable `ModelSelector` component. This ensures consistency and better code organization.

Adds model selection functionality to the App Builder screen. It now maintains state for the selected model, displays the reusable `ModelSelector` component in its header, and passes the selected model to the `aiService` when generating code.

Creates a new, reusable `ModelSelector` React component. This component renders a dropdown menu for selecting an AI model, which can be easily embedded in different parts of the application to ensure a consistent user experience.

payload.text: Enhance the chat interface to automatically detect and highlight code blocks within AI responses using a library like 'react-syntax-highlighter'. Support multiple programming languages.

**Analyzing Code Highlighting**

I'm focusing on automatically detecting and highlighting code blocks in the chat interface. It's a key UI/UX enhancement. I've realized that `react-syntax-highlighter` is already in use within the codebase, specifically in `components/Chat`, which is promising. I'm exploring how to best integrate it for automated detection and rendering of code blocks.


**Reviewing Implementation Details**

I'm now diving deeper into the existing `ChatScreen.tsx` component. It appears `react-syntax-highlighter` is already integrated using the `Prism` style. The `ReactMarkdown` component is using `remarkGfm` and the code block component is customized to use `SyntaxHighlighter`. I'll examine the custom code block component to understand how code language detection and highlighting are managed within the current setup.


**Confirming Existing Integration**

I've confirmed that the core integration is already in place within `ChatScreen.tsx`.  The `Message` component leverages `ReactMarkdown` with `remarkGfm` and custom `code` components to render highlighted code blocks. It employs `react-syntax-highlighter` using the `Prism` style, and language detection is already handled.  I'm now satisfied this satisfies the user's initial request.


**Verifying Feature Compliance**

I've examined the implementation in `ChatScreen.tsx` closely. `ReactMarkdown` with `remarkGfm` handles automatic code block detection. The custom `code` component, employing `react-syntax-highlighter` using the `vscDarkPlus` theme, supports syntax highlighting for specified languages, matching the request precisely. The initial goal is fulfilled, but maybe the user is wanting more?


**Refining Existing Implementation**

I've re-examined the implementation, finding it already addresses the core requirements using `react-syntax-highlighter` within the `ChatScreen` component. Now, I'm focusing on enhancements. The current dark theme, `vscDarkPlus`, works fine, but I'll make it adapt to light mode too. I'll also add a header to code blocks, displaying the language, and improve the overall code block container's styling for a better UI.


**Implementing Theme Switching**

I've realized the core feature is already functional: code blocks auto-detect and highlight using `react-syntax-highlighter` in the `ChatScreen` component. To elevate this, I'm focusing on dynamic theme switching between a dark and light mode for the code blocks, using `vscDarkPlus` for dark mode, and researching a light theme such as `oneLight`. I will integrate this with the app's theme context. I'm also planning to improve the overall code block design.

I will implement a better code block header, language display, and a more polished UI with rounded corners and distinct backgrounds to enhance its visual integration within the app's glassmorphism style. The plan is also to move the "copy" button within the new header, for better organization.

I've examined the imports and existing structure more. The focus is now on implementing dynamic theme switching within `ChatScreen.tsx` for code highlighting. I'll import `oneLight` from `react-syntax-highlighter/dist/esm/styles/prism` and leverage the app's theme context. I'll add a header for language display and style the code blocks appropriately. The "copy" button will be moved into the new code block header.

I'm focusing on styling code blocks, adding a header for the language, and moving the "copy" button into this header. I'm enhancing the UI for a glassmorphism style.

The `CodeBlock` component will handle the rendering, with a header, language display, and copy button. I'll focus on styling, including rounded corners and background adjustments, for a more polished presentation.

This component will encapsulate the code block rendering, header, language display, and the copy button, improving overall code maintainability and user experience.  I'm focusing on UI styling to match the application's design, integrating  glassmorphism and enhancing the copy button functionality.

I'm focusing on dynamic theme integration via `useTheme` for the code highlighter, and I will add a language header with a copy button. I'm also styling for rounded corners and a distinct background to integrate it within the glassmorphism UI.

I'll focus on UI styling and the copy button's integration within the `CodeBlock` header.

I will leverage the `useTheme` hook with `oneLight` and `vscDarkPlus` to ensure the code block UI adapts to light and dark themes, including a new header and moving the copy button. I'm also focusing on styling to match the app's glassmorphism UI.

I've imported both `oneLight` and `vscDarkPlus`, and I'm leveraging `useTheme` for theme context. I'm focusing on UI styling to improve integration and adding the copy button functionality within the header of the code block.

The `CodeBlock` will handle rendering with a header, language display, and a copy button. The UI includes a header with the language and copy button, complemented by UI enhancements with rounded corners and a distinct background.

**Implementing Dynamic Theme Switching**

I've now incorporated the `CodeBlock` component for organized code highlighting within `ChatScreen.tsx`. This component dynamically switches between themes using the `useTheme` hook, with `oneLight` for light mode and `vscDarkPlus` for dark mode.  The UI is enhanced with a header containing the language and a copy button. I'm focusing on refining UI details to match the overall design, specifically rounding corners and improving background clarity.

Of course! I've enhanced the chat interface to significantly improve how code is displayed. The previous implementation had basic highlighting, but I've upgraded it with a more polished and feature-rich presentation.

Here are the key improvements I've made to the AI's responses in the chat:

1.  **Theme-Aware Syntax Highlighting**: Code blocks now automatically switch between a light or dark theme to match the rest of the application, ensuring perfect readability in any mode.
2.  **Dedicated Code Component**: I've created a new, specialized `CodeBlock` component to beautifully render code snippets.
3.  **Informative Header**: Each code block now has a clean header that displays the detected programming language (e.g., "javascript", "python").
4.  **Integrated Copy Button**: The "Copy code" button has been moved into the header for a more intuitive and organized user experience.
5.  **Improved Styling**: The overall look and feel of both block and inline code has been refined to better match the app's modern, glassmorphism aesthetic.

This enhancement makes reading and interacting with code in your chat conversations a much more pleasant and professional experience. I've updated the `ChatScreen.tsx` component to include these changes.

entries.description: Enhances the chat interface by introducing a sophisticated `CodeBlock` component for syntax highlighting. This new component is theme-aware, switching between light and dark styles automatically. It also features a header displaying the code's language and an integrated copy button for a better user experience. The styling for both block-level and inline code has been improved to match the app's aesthetic.
